[03:05] It's not necessary. There's a lot of cut like cut corners that you can take whether it's getting with a mentor or studying the right books. Just make sure that it always comes from a credible source.
[03:18] When I first started selling, I was actually selling in the state of Florida and I just had a personal training gig at you. We didn't have a sales director and sales director was this high high ranking position where someone had to have a college degree and be suitable in exercise science in order to sell training.

So we're one of eight gyms that's selling in this this district. We didn't have a director at the time and I just got hired on and I didn't have any fucking clients to train.
[03:58] So I did what I knew to do, hustle and go up and like floorpool people. We ended up being the highest grossing gym in the district and the only one without a director. So I drew some unnecessary eyes and it was very unintentional, but it drew a lot of eyes like towards me and what I was doing.

[04:55] I'm like looking at that money, right? And then I'm looking at my paycheck and like, "Wow, this is really fucking shit. Why am I doing this?" Made no sense. Well, I was making all this money for these people, but only getting like such a small percentage.
[05:10] So, I quit. I don't know that was the smartest thing to do at the time because I sure shit didn't have a fucking plan. Also, one of the things that we're going to get into, the whole commit first and then figure it out later.

[18:03] And I needed to find a way to get in with somebody that if I was getting necessary information in the right field, I needed to make sure that before I started anything, I could connect the dots around other people that were in the field and also successful. So I could combine the knowledge.
[18:23] That way I wasn't making pitfalls or finding them myself because there was no way to climb out of that fucking hole other than just, you know, eating the shit that I pretty much shoveled into my own face. I probably could have found a way to fix that.

[27:38] So you can create that pinnacle and that structure that you want which also means that you make sure that you double down like Mike was saying infrastructure has to be there in order to take care of your clients. That's super super important.
[27:53] So there's a limited belief is like selling is what you do to people like you do all like these manipulation tactics. It's not selling is not what you do to people. Selling is what you do for people. You got me?
[28:10] And that's why the framing and the seemingly trying things, the things that seem like manipulation tactics, they're not. They're like for a reframing process. So when you embed belief systems in people or you change their belief system, that's ultimately how you actually get people to buy.
[28:28] If not, I'm just sitting and I'm convincing you to do something. Convincing and persuasion are completely two different things. By definition, they are not the same thing. If I convince you to do something, it's for my own good, my needs, and what I want.
[28:45] If I persuade you to do something, I'm persuading you to do something for your benefit. Completely different things. And taking that account into information before we start getting into some of the things that we can kind of knock out, the formulas that would start to integrate into our sales process for our sales teams and our setters.

[30:35] How you change subconscious beliefs is exactly what Jeremy said before you get up here. You actually embed a belief system in them and then you get them to integrate it into their prior beliefs which actually formulates and changes it to them.
[30:50] So, by the time you get to the end of your pitch, they're convincing you that they need to buy, not the other way around. And there's a framework to implement this belief system. And it works every fucking single time. Every time.

[34:10] The marketers always bitching that the sales teams can't close and like the sales teams are always bitching like the marketers can't get qualified leads and like it's like this fucking it's like a marriage, right? Where they're just kind of like going back and forth all the time.

[35:00] The other half of you need to get on it so you can make a lot more fucking money because I promise you if you're good at what you do, you'll make a lot more money. How many of you guys are good at what you do? All right, a lot of you make more money. Cool. All right, sweet.
[35:18] This helps, by the way, too. I want to backtrack on this. Uh, if you guys have problems getting qualified leads in, there is a priming process that you guys can go through. It's, and again, this is just this is coming from me and and what's worked for us.
[35:35] I know this kind of like negates what Jeremy said before he came up here. I found a way to pivot the process a little bit. Some people have setters, some people don't. I substituted this in for a setter framework. I do a qualification um application.
[35:53] However, I only make it like five or six questions. I do a priming pre-frame and then I go into these four things. This was made by IBM in the 1950s. It works fucking fantastic.

[49:50] And everybody thinks like you got to cast like these voodoo words in order to get people to like put pen on a paper and send you over money. And what's crazy is it's literally the least amount of the communication that's transmitted to the person that's perceiving what you're saying, what you're doing, and what your body language is.
[50:10] 55% of what you communicate comes from your body language. 38% comes from your the vindiction in your tonality, how well you deliver if you're confident in what you're saying. [54:28] Art's like probably one of the best things that I can use as an example. I still don't understand why fucking some art pieces are priced the way they are. [55:08] Extrinsic is like market price. Perceived and intrinsic value is where you set the price for delivered availability. So when they don't understand what your offer is and what you have, they will check the price tag around it.

[66:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Closer Formula"
NÚMEROS: Not specified
VISUAL: Framework principal de fechamento, sistema completo de vendas
[66:43] All right, so here's the formula. Call it the closer formula. Who's really fucking excited for the closer formula? Okay. Who's really excited for closer formula? Let's go.
[67:00] All right. All right. So some of the best positioning questions when we can do going through closer formulas when we're actually sitting in sales and we're selling to our prospect clarity questions is reiterating one of the questions that come from Bant framework to instantly build rapport.
[67:20] And this is after you've you know seemingly introduced yourself you guys build a little rapport outside of that but like why are they In a perfect world, what's the best case scenario for them? All right, more importantly, you if you guys are being asked later on your pitch, why should I go with you?
[67:40] Instead of going with them, you ask them first. Why did you schedule with us? Why particularly us? What drew you us to book the call? Why are we sitting down today? Sweet. Well, now that I know you got that self-belief, you tell me what the expectation is. That way, I can give it to you. Objection already done. They solved it themselves.
[68:03] Label them with a problem. Every single time you go into a sales pitch, you have to solve a problem. This is how you talk to a subconscious mind. You change belief systems. Sometimes people don't give us problems, especially if we clarify where they're there. People don't want to open up to salespeople.
[68:23] We get a bad name, whatever. And maybe sometimes we are just a bunch of sleazy salesmen throwing shit over the phone. Whatever. I deliver a product service that's quality. So, I justify myself there. But you need to identify the issue and what they're communicating.
[68:40] And that's where we reimplement the active listening that we were talking about. It's super key and very, very, very important. And then you want to readress the problem back into them so you can make sure like you understood them correctly.
[68:58] Because if you do that now, they're not just telling you it, you're communicating it back to them and they're confirming it out loud. This is how you embed subconscious thoughts. They give you the answer, you make them say it back.
[69:15] And if they haven't given you one, you use one and three together. The overview past experiences. So if I can clarify where they're there and then I overview their past experience, I can formulate a problem to give them. It might not even actually be a problem. It could just be one that I throw out and sticks.
[69:35] But now I can actually give them solutions for said problem, which means I've solved an issue. What you got? So after they clarify that so like if I clarify with somebody. We'll just um I I would actually rather not go with one of mine. I'll go with somebody else's.
[69:55] That way I can give you guys something of value you guys can walk away with and execute on. Actually, do do any of you guys ever sell anything passive? Do you guys any of you guys sell passive income? Any kind of like e-commerce, automation, anything? Any you guys in automation space? No.
[70:15] Okay. Yeah, we could stick that course then. What do you What do you sell? We're we're like so our offer is anywhere from 30 to 100. Okay. So, what's what's the goal for your your like um I don't want to say like dream client, but like who's your client attribute that you want to take on and and like what's your average goal for them to get to?
[70:40] Yeah. Usually doing 600,000 outcomes in the first six months over the course of six months or each month over the course of six months a million to 3 million a month.
[70:55] Okay. So you clarifying why they're there would essentially be reasking the questions um why are you sitting here today and why more more specifically why did you book with me or why did you consult with me and the reason why you want to ask those questions is because it's going to give you some kind of lenency.
[71:15] Even if they sidestep it and they just say well I just maybe I just wanted to shop around okay so are you currently with somebody else right now like is that why you're shopping or like are you trying to come away from and they're going to say yes.
[71:33] Can you maybe shed a little bit of light on like what's gone wrong over there and why you feel like you need to turn to pivot? Because sometimes, and we're going to get to a part and and this can be executed early on if this situation actually arises.
[71:50] When people feel like they're being sold, they don't want to buy. People want to buy because they want to buy. People literally buy for one reason and one reason only. Desire. That's it. You have to accumulate desire in them. That's the whole point embedding in subconscious. And we're going to get there, right?
[72:10] So, if I understand why they're leaving somebody else and they didn't want to tell me that right out the gate, this is where I like reframe. Well, I want to make sure like they're doing something that we're also not doing because I don't want this to be like a thing where I deliver the same service and you're still unhappy.
[72:30] There's always got to be a mutual qualification process here. So, I know you want us to be a right fit, but now I got to actually ask you some questions to make sure you're a right fit from us because we don't actually take on all of our clients.
[72:48] We haven't got to that part of the process, but when we get to more the end of the sales pitch, if this is something you decide to do, I'm going to ask you some qualifications to make sure like you are a right fit and we can even sign you on.
[73:05] And all of a sudden, they're like, "Oh, fuck. All right, so you might not take my money. What the fuck do I got to answer?" And all it's going to be is like literally your client attribute. Do you guys have client attributes? Jeremy talked about this like from the beginning of time. You guys have those set aside? Do you guys all know what they are?
[73:25] You ask three of those questions at the end, which by the way should all be no-brainers. And then it's that easy to slide the contract over. You don't even ask about at that point. You don't even talk about cost or ask. You literally tell them that they qualified and you're glad that it was a mutual fit. You slide the paper over.
[73:48] You will be fucking so surprised on how many people don't even blink. Where where can I wire the money? They know tried like proven and true. I've turned people away. Like I've literally turned people away. Especially people being rude. Like I don't I don't deal with like rude people.
[74:08] Like I'll shut that off well before I ever take a payment. I've hung up on people and I've had them text me apologizing and I literally collected the fucking 45K wires that day. I think it was Will's first day on the job. It's all framing man.
[74:28] It's manipulative a little bit like tactics, but again, I know that I'm doing something good. I'm not doing it out of bad intention. I just know how the process works. Mhm. All right. Who's here with a partner? We're going to actually try to do this one. How'd that feel? Right. Yeah,
[74:50] It's really really easy to like get the information that you need that everybody's like it's it's really weird. Uh the other common misconception is like everybody needs to know like a plethora of like closes. Like what's the close that I got to say to get over this objection?
[75:08] Like you don't need anything to say to get over the objection. Your sales pitch fucking sucks and you need to start over, bro. Like all this is done like prior. That way when you get there and even one even slightly starts to come up, I already have information to throw back at them.
[75:28] Because necessarily they said something along the lines of their wife wasn't involved or their wife had already acknowledged the fact that they were on the call because they're really close and you guys connected over the fact that you and your wife talk about everything, which by the way I've used a hundred fucking thousand times.
[75:48] And that'll actually help me get to like more of whatever the issue is. And like all right, let's be honest with you, man. I think the offer is really great and no, I didn't have to talk to my wife. I just don't necessarily have the money. Oh, bro, let me help you out with it.
[76:05] I have a lending program that I can set up with you. It's literally 2.7%. Also, when you start accumulating wealth, you learn very early on, you don't spend your own money. You spend the bank's money. Let me help you spend the bank's money. That shit just gets smoother and smoother the more you practice it. No one says no to that. No one says no to that.
[76:28] They feel embarrassed a lot of times when they don't actually have money and it is quality product. That's why I said it's very important that you get get set up with a lending company. If you really want to help somebody, help them. Also, the other thing, that's another reason why I put out free content.
[76:48] The best way for people to find you and know that you can help them is by actually helping them, right?
[76:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Sell The Solution Positioning"
NÚMEROS: Not specified
VISUAL: Posicionamento de produto, diferenciação vs competição
[76:58] All right. Next part of the framework, sell the solution positioning. When it comes to company positioning, a lot of you guys sell similar shit. You need to find out how you can sell different shit or sell what you're selling and position it as different shit.
[77:18] The reason why is because this is another one of those saying that's been thrown at us from like the very beginning of the time that the grass isn't always greener on the other side. And a lot of times maybe it's not. However, people want greener grass. They don't want more of the same.
[77:38] If they wanted more of the same, they wouldn't be there talking to you. There's a big point to that. Anytime you market or you sell or you have a position or maybe it's a guaranteed offer like something that makes you unique and we go back down to like the USP part of the process.
[77:58] Better is great. You can have better numbers and better analytics. But again, to redouble back down on it, it's not about being better. It's about being different. Different is better than better. Different draws interest in intrigue. Intrigue is tied into emotion.
[78:18] If I can get them to emotionally justify why they're going to buy and I have intrigue now involved, I'm hand over fist going to always collect their money. That make sense? I tried to slow that one down because I know that's like a fucking mouthful and I got cotton mouth bad. Mhm. It's because of you.
[78:38] All right. Explain the service. So I put this more towards the end of the framework and closer just seem to work out perfect. There's sometimes legalities or like contract stipulations, things that do need to be communicated, but you need to build all of the emotion prior to actually getting into doing this.
[78:58] Because this will push people away a lot of times before you can actually get them to justify why they need it first. This is how things work. These are how things operate. um maybe create a timeline. And then a lot of times too, you can actually use use what people would feel like would be negatives.
[79:18] Like we have a uh we have a current client that doesn't do a he doesn't do a money back guarantee. And I pitched a different guarantee style and we argued over like the board. And the pitch was, we're not going to do a money back guarantee because the people that they they use the platform has more of a say so than what they're allowed to, even if they follow guidelines.
[79:40] If you guys have ever dealt with Amazon, you know exactly what the fuck I'm talking about. I mean, sometimes with Facebook, you guys still know what I'm talking about. Their pitch is if a store gets locked up and they can't get the store out, even if they've given all the necessary information, we've got them agreed to launch a new account.
[80:00] So, the guarantee is no, it's not a money back guarantee. You've bought into passive income, and that's exactly what we're going to do to you. I feel like it would be a terrible thing 10 months down the road to give you exactly the amount of money that you gave me, and now I feel like I've borrowed it and let you down and not giving you what you wanted.
[80:20] At that point, I relaunch your account and I double down on the account and I make you more money in a passive stream of income because that's what you originally bought into. That's what I guarantee is going to happen.
[80:35] Bomb dropped. You always need to find a way to pivot. If that means that you need to get a little bit more unique or quickwitted on how you can spend certain things that are in your contract, find a way that you can position it as a positive and you'll know you'll have no negatives in your contract. None.
[80:55] There's always a reposition. Always a reposition. It's the biggest thing I've drilled into those three sitting the back of the couch. They get pressed on those questions all the time. And the very first thing I ask is, "How did you make it positive?" And they come up with some really good shit sometimes. Then I get really proud. Yeah, it's nice.
[81:15] All right, we talked about that. Cool.
[81:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Reinforcing A Decision"
NÚMEROS: Not specified
VISUAL: Processo de reforço pós-venda, garantindo satisfação do cliente
[81:20] All right, so sometimes when we get into like reinforcing a decision, it's the last part of the framework. No one ever ever ever wants to leave a sale feeling like they were sold. You always want to leave somebody with a positive mind versus a weary mind.
[81:38] It's also how you get returning customers. It's also how you make sure that whoever left is telling them about an experience and now you're getting a referral or an affiliate. And there's components to being able to do this.
[81:55] So a lot of times if you guys have addressed time frame for expectations implementing BL BN or going through closer framework, you know what it is that they're looking for. You just want them to recommunicate it just like what we were just talking about. I do need to get them to say it.
[82:13] So, if I can deliver you this by so and so time frame, which is what they communicated to me, sometimes my service can do it faster, I'm going to ask them. So, if I can do it by this, that would be great. But would you be opposed if I could do this three or four months faster? Would it be okay if we did that?
[82:35] Abso-fucking-lutely. Why would they ever have a problem with it being an underpromise and an over deliver? You're always going to have a happy client. They're going to feel more confident putting their signature on that paper. It happens every single time. Every time.
[82:53] If a reschedule is absolutely necessary for whatever reason and there is a lending company involved and they do have to fill out an application, it is very very important that your closers or setters, whoever that you have set up with, follows a three-step process.
[83:13] If there's average chart numbers, some kind of projections, or a testimonial that's specifically tied to whatever their story is or what they're trying to achieve, you need to have it loaded and ready.
[83:28] And the text template has something to do with a note that you've taken down from your guys' pitch talking to them that's was a specific concern with them. This person reminded me of you. I just want to make sure I could send this over to you. That way you can kind of get an idea of like how this runs from somebody that was in your particular situation.
[83:50] Now it's relatable, right? It's competent and there's a bridge gap. There's no money collected yet. I don't care who says that they're going to buy. If the money is not collected yet, it's not collected yet. It's not sale. That means you're not done with the sales process.
[84:08] Notes should be taken during your pitch. Again, something I push really hard with them. How many times has this helped you guys close? Taking something personal from somebody, figuring out if they had a daughter had a dance recital or maybe a family member was ill, maybe something happened to like their cat or fucking dog.
[84:30] And you I I don't know, right? But you follow up with them, even if it's a week, two weeks later, and that's the very first thing you say on the phone. That's not what they're expecting. They're thinking you're following up with them wanting to know when they're going to pay.
[84:48] No, now you genuinely care about the person. It completely drops their frame. Now you can bridge into I'm glad that everything's better now, and I'm I'm happy to hear that. I was just calling to follow up, but So, where do we stand as far as being able to move forward?
[85:08] Do you think you need to take some more time that way you can cope and deal with that or do you want me to send the paperwork back through and maybe we can get started and fix that issue or did you want to take more time or do you want to start making results now?
[85:25] Strong power words for ending frame. So I drop the frame so it's less aggressive and then I deliver more aggressively. Does that make sense? Fucking sweet. I like you guys a lot.
[85:40] All right, last thing there should always be uh and I saw the finger go up, so this is kind of like perfect. I rushed as much as I could. Sorry, bro. Um, you always want to congratulate a client on their future success and decision. Always. That's like the seal the deal thing.
[86:00] If you can leave them with something too, like any kind of like educational material or parting material or maybe even if you sign them on like a bigger package and you want to welcome them into being a part of a community and you guys are trying to build some rapport, send something specific that's like brand niche to your company.
[86:20] Like if you guys are doing something like a for like a photo editor or like a picture like do a dope concept where you actually take a picture sit in a fucking box or a manila manila envelope or and then depending on who the person is like if they like something cater a little product in there.
[86:40] I don't know how much they're paying you 10 15k right for your video. It cost you like fucking 20 to 30 bucks to do that. That's like a power branding move that makes people feel so much happier knowing that they signed up with somebody that actually gives a fuck about them.
[87:00] And again will reflect and then referring other people in other businesses. One person catapults into 10 really, really, really quick, especially if you build good rapport. All right, I had more shit but that'll make you a lot of money. So, go make a lot of money.
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
$220,000/mês em receita recorrente (academia)
$36,000 economias iniciais
$24,000 investimento em equipamento de piso
$196,000 potencial de retorno (oportunidade perdida)
$10,000 curso Amazon
$17,000 em 14 dias (Amazon)
62 dias de freeze na conta Amazon
48x ROAS no último mês
$1.6M em receita
$32-33K em gastos
67.8% taxa de fechamento em ofertas high-ticket
$100K+ ofertas de alto valor
$550K pacote de posicionamento (5x maior)
$45K serviços típicos
620 score de crédito mínimo
2.6% APR em financiamento
$5,000 vs $50,000 diferença de cliente
$100 demonstração ao vivo
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
BANT Framework (Budget, Authority, Need, Time) - IBM 1950s
3 C's Framework: Connect, Correct, Collect
Closer Formula - sistema completo de fechamento
7-38-55 Principle (Dr. Albert Mehrabian, 1960s)
7% palavras
38% tonalidade
55% linguagem corporal
Qualification Application (5-6 perguntas)
Priming, Delivery, Follow-up framework
Positioning Package Strategy
Lending/Funding integration
Active listening vs listening to respond
Subconscious belief embedding
Story-based selling vs feature-based
Mutual qualification process
Underpromise, overdeliver strategy
Personal note-taking during pitch
3-step follow-up process
Referral and upgrade systems
ESTRUTURA:
USP (Unique Sales Proposition) obrigatório
Múltiplas ofertas (downsell, upsell, exclusividade)
Pacote de posicionamento 5x+ do principal
Sistema de lending integrado
Revshare deals preferencial
Sales + Marketing alignment crítico
Client attributes definidos
Professional presentation setup
Quality audio/video equipment
Branded office space
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
High-ticket → High-fulfillment reframe
Preço nunca deve ser objeção
Financiamento até $100K disponível
2.6% APR para clientes qualificados
Clientes $50K+ = menos problemas, mais lucro
Clientes $5K = 500 ligações/dia
ROI de tempo > ROI de dinheiro
Posicionamento de valor intrínseco vs extrínseco
Exemplo: bolsa Jansport $50 vs Louis Vuitton $50,200
1-2 clientes "loucos"/mês compram pacote premium = $1M+/mês
DURAÇÃO: ~87 minutos FORMATO: Apresentação ao vivo / Workshop interativo com demonstrações práticas
